Then over time their team leaders get frustrated with stupid ideas filtering down from incompetent, out of touch upper management and having to try implement those dumb ideas with the DEI dead weight hires so they quit. Then all you have left are the DEI hires who dont know sht about fk and no one can fire them because they're disabled or some other sht. 

Basically MBAs ruin everything once again. Honestly, those...let's say "people", shouldnt ever have anything to do with creative industries.

replies.author: @Doomweapon66
replies.text:
@bluemoondm8629 I myself have played Destiny since the D1 beta when one of my friends had an extra code for it and asked if I wanted to try it and I went all the way to the end of Final Shape. I can tell you firsthand that Destiny-era Bungie has *always* had a problem with not listening to player feedback to the point that "we're listening" became an active meme in the Destiny community, even amongst the content creators and streamers. And a lot of Destiny-era Bungie's own problems with Destiny 2 in particular have been their own fault and I'll give you a couple examples of why (and subsequently point out why nobody should have faith in "new Marathon" based on "new Bungie's" balancing track record).

Example 1: The infamous tale of Dead Man's Tale in D2. Dead Man's Tale on it's launch was something I and my roughly 6 other friends who played D2 a lot back in the day remember very vividly. Dead Man's Tale on launch was able to 2 shot headshot in PvP on average (I spoke to one of my friends on this the other day when we were talking about "new Marathon" and he said it was even possible to 1 shot people with headshots with Dead Man's Tale if you got it's perk stacked with a couple other things) and it fires at roughly 140 RPM IIRC when hip firing. The entire theming of the weapon is it's a "cowboy repeater" and you're supposed to hip fire it and go for headshots.
Now, shortly after it was released and after people were finding ways to easily 2 shot people with basically no skill at all (the aim assist in D2 has always either been really regarded and OP or halfway useless), "new Bungie" nerfed the Dead Man's Tale to make it a 3 shot kill instead (2 headshots and I believe a body shot IIRC). The problem is Dead Man's does not fire fast enough to be able to make 3 shot kills with it a feasible option (assuming your opponent either has good enough accuracy, a faster firing weapon, or both). 
This basically kept going infinitely back and forth because "new Bungie" made the "mistake" of making a weapon that didn't fire fast enough to be a 3 shot kill but it being a 2 shot kill completely breaks the PvP sandbox because it requires very little actual skill to use. IIRC their "middle ground" was attempting to nerf the aim assist but that just went back to a similar issue of either needing to be way too accurate with your headshots or it just not requiring skill anymore. This illustrates that "new Bungie's" design principles just suck because they make weapons that are cool but then it comes at the cost of balance. And it'd be one thing in my mind if they took a Borderlands or a Titanfall 2 type of approach where they just make the guns all really strong and say screw balancing but "new Bungie" wants to "have their cake and eat it too."

Example 2: DPS phases. This one is best illustrated with what happened with Final Shape's exotic Golden Gun sniper rifle in my eyes. The Golden Gun sniper was a very fun weapon and it had a really nice synergy with the Celestial Nighthawk, a hunter class exotic that made your Golden Gun only have 1 shot but it's damage would massively increase in exchange. The Golden Gun sniper was affected by Celestial Nighthawk's perk and with how you charge the Golden Gun shots on the sniper (which was headshots or headshot kills or something like that IIRC), you can use both the sniper and your actual Golden Gun for some good damage on basically whatever (basically a giant f off button for when you needed it).
Fast forward to the Final Shape raid release and one of the things that became a really common place DPS strategy was using several hunters in a team all equipped with the sniper and Celestial Nighthawk Golden Guns for DPS phases. This damage strategy was extremely powerful for how simple it was to execute (tbh, the hardest part of it all was using several hunters and not keeling over to mobs, which based necessitated some form of consistent healing). 
What happened shortly after this became a DPS strat that extended into every raid DPS phase across the game was Bungie nerfing the sniper (I think they either nerfed the charge rate or the damage it would get form the synergy with Celestial). This ended up basically destroying the strategy almost entirely because the damage just wasn't as good anymore, which both artificially pads out the boss fights you brought the strat to and it artificially increases difficulty by making you take more time and increasing your chances of keeling over in a battle of attrition (mind you, D2 has always been extremely inconsistent about giving you ammo and I've had to deal with DPS phases for bosses when I had no heavy or special ammo going in because the game just wouldn't drop it). 
The ultimate end result is raiding became niche yet again because the "skill floors" artificially increased yet again because of arbitrary artificial difficulty increases via nerfing damage you can put out and making you have to contend with the possibility of the game just telling you to f yourself even more. 

For reference on what I mean with the artificial difficulty, I can't even give a "ballpark estimate" count of the amount of times me and my raid teams have wiped before just because we didn't get enough people to get enough ammo drops between DPS phases or because somebody keeled over because *1* random red bar fallen dreg or whatever shot the guy twice and just did all of the person's health within those 2 shots. Virtually every single person I have ever played Destiny 2 with (and probably even D2 players I've never met) can all attest to this being something that just happens and there's nothing you, the player, can necessarily do other than nuke the enemy or get some form of healing to *try* and offset it.
I should also note that the latter of those 2 things is an extremely consistent problem going all the way back to Destiny 1 even, where sometimes a random open world enemy will just look at you having a total of 200 HP (source: D2 PvP health values at 100 resilience stat from when I last played was 200 HP and I can't see "new Bungie" making that value technically any higher on the backend for PvE when you don't have a numerical health value shown anywhere), shoot you twice, and you're left at maybe 10 HP from that. 
I'm used to dealing with difficult enemies who do all sorts of BS (i.e. Dark Souls 3 Nameless King actively annoys the hell out of me because his first phase with the dragon is a great example of where the Souls game camera system is complete garbage against enemies who have any significant vertical movements). That said, I argue the Souls games themselves and even adjacent stuff (i.e. Code Vein 1 my first time through was *rough* and that game's combat gets really rough if you're like me and can't parry anything to save your life) are infinitely more fair that what you get in D2's hardest endgame stuff because sometimes you can stack the 30% or whatever exact value it is out of the DR from resilience stat (at least that's what it gave last time I played) with armor mods for DR and everything and the enemies will still randomly 1-2 shot you even through all of that.

I'll be good faith here at the end and say *maybe* "new Bungie" will balance "new Marathon" better than they did with D2. I don't believe it will because it's the exact same devs and their balancing track with D2 was absolutely terrible and inconsistent but I'll be infinitely more generous than they deserve from me at this point and say give them time to prove how they will balance "new Marathon." I very much suspect it'll go the same way balancing D2 went but it could always be different from the reality.
